1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
80	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
81	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
82	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
83	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
84	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
88	available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the structure-sheaf unit are present as
89	scheme-level objects. Rather than the geometric local-triviality route, the
90	group law is built on \(\otimes\)-\emph{invertibility}: an object is a line
91	object when it admits a tensor inverse, \(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong
92	\mathcal{O}_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), exactly Mathlib's
93	\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom. Under this predicate requirement (3)
94	becomes the three monoidal \emph{coherence isomorphisms} of \(\otimes_X\) ---
95	associator, unitors and braiding --- each obtained cheaply: the unitors and
96	braiding as \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) of the corresponding
97	presheaf-level isomorphism, and the associator (only needed on the invertible
98	--- hence flat --- objects the law multiplies) as a three-step composite that
99	absorbs the two nested sheafifications by applying the sheafification functor to
100	the unit morphism whiskered by a flat object, with the presheaf-level associator
101	transported in between, while the inverse is carried by the predicate itself.
102	These
103	assemble the commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes whose units group is
104	the Picard group (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The geometric
105	compatibility isomorphism \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\)
106	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and any separate tensor-inverse lemma are
107	\emph{off} this critical path. The full symmetric monoidal category on
108	\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is also \emph{not} required; see
109	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
110	
111	\section{Mathlib API survey}
112	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
113	
114	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
115	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
116	
117	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
118	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
119	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
120	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
121	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
122	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
123	sectionwise tensor product
124	\[
125	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
126	\]
127	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
128	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
129	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
130	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
131	
132	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
133	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
134	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
135	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
136	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
137	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
138	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
139	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
140	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
141	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
142	tensor-product operation
143	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
144	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
145	
146	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
147	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
148	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
149	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
150	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
151	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The data this operation must supply for the group law are the monoidal
152	\emph{coherence isomorphisms} of \(\otimes_X\) --- the associator
153	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), the left/right unitors
154	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}) and the braiding
155	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The unitors and braiding are obtained by the
156	cheap pattern \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) applied to the corresponding
157	presheaf-level isomorphism on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\); the associator,
158	restricted to the \(\otimes\)-invertible --- hence flat --- objects the group
159	law actually multiplies, is obtained as the three-step composite of
160	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: apply the sheafification functor to the unit
161	morphism whiskered by a flat object to absorb each of the two nested
162	sheafifications (once on each side), with the presheaf-level associator
163	transported by \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) in between. The only non-formal
164	input is the flat-whiskering bridge \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}: whiskering
165	a sheafification-localizer morphism by a flat object stays in the localizer
166	class, which holds objectwise by flatness (local injectivity from
167	\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\), local
168	surjectivity from right-exactness of \(\otimes\)). Neither route uses the
169	abstract \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\) adjoint, a
170	\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure on presheaves of modules, or a bundled
171	\(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) instance on the localizer class.
172	Together with the
173	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
174	(which carries the inverse existentially) these assemble the commutative monoid
175	of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units group
176	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The project does \emph{not} require a
177	global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance on all quasi-coherent
178	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure:
179	monoidal coherence beyond these three isomorphisms is never consumed by a group
180	law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}). The
181	open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
182	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is \emph{not} on this critical path and is
183	retained only as an optional supplement.
184	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
185	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
186	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
187	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
188	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
189	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
190	independently of any upstream timing.
191	
192	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
193	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
194	
195	\begin{definition}
196	
197	\leanok
198	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
199	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
200	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
201	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
202	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
203	  \[
204	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
205	  \]
206	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
207	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
208	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
209	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
210	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
211	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
212	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
213	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
214	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
215	  canonical compatibility
216	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
217	     \;\cong\;
218	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
219	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
220	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
221	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
222	
223	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
224	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
225	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
226	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
227	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
228	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
229	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
230	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
231	\end{definition}
232	
233	\begin{lemma}
234	
235	\leanok
236	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
237	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
238	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
239	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
240	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
241	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
242	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
243	  \[
244	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
245	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
246	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
247	  \]
248	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
249	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
250	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
251	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
252	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
253	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
254	  \[
255	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
256	    \qquad
257	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
258	  \]
259	  the associator
260	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
261	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
262	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
263	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
264	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
265	\end{lemma}
266	
267	\begin{proof}
268	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
269	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
270	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
271	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
272	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
273	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
274	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
275	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
276	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
277	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
278	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
279	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
280	\end{proof}
281	
282	\begin{remark}
283	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
284	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
285	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
286	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
287	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
288	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
289	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
290	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
291	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
292	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
293	  depend on it.
294	
295	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
296	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
297	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
298	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
299	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
300	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
301	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
302	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
303	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
304	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
305	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
306	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
307	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
308	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
309	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
310	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
311	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
312	
313	  Crucially, the group law restricts that stability to \emph{flat} whiskering
314	  objects, where it becomes elementary and needs no closed structure: for
315	  \emph{flat} \(F\) the implication \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) is
316	  exactly the bridge \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}, proved objectwise from
317	  flatness (local injectivity via
318	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\), local
319	  surjectivity via right-exactness of \(\otimes\)). Since every
320	  \(\otimes\)-invertible module is flat, this flat-scoped stability is all the
321	  group law's associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}) consumes, and it
322	  sidesteps the \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) wall that the arbitrary-\(F\) version
323	  would hit.
324	% NOTE: (iter-212, review) "every ⊗-invertible module is flat" is the SAME gap
325	% flagged in detail at the proof of lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso below. The bridge
326	% needs SECTIONWISE flatness over EVERY open (∀ U, Module.Flat 𝒪_X(U) (F.val U)),
327	% which global ⊗-invertibility does NOT supply and which is false over a
328	% non-affine open. The fix is local-triviality whiskering, not global flatness.
329	
330	  The group law needs only the coherence isomorphisms
331	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}:
332	  the unitors and braiding by \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) of the
333	  presheaf-level isomorphisms, and the associator by the flat-whiskering
334	  three-step composite above. None of these is built from the off-path
335	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
336	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
337	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
338	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
339	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
340	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
341	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
342	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
343	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
344	\end{remark}

346	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
347	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
348	
349	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
350	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
351	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
352	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
353	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
354	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the restriction-compatibility
355	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
356	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
357	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law
358	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
359	cover, together with their restriction to the
360	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
361	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
362	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\
363	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
364	
365	\begin{lemma}
366	
367	\leanok
368	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
369	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
370	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
371	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
372	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
373	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
374	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
375	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
376	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
377	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
378	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
379	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
380	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
381	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
382	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
383	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
384	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
385	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
386	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
387	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
388	  \[
389	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
390	      \;\;:=\;\;
391	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
392	  \]
393	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
394	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
395	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
396	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
397	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
398	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
399	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
400	\end{lemma}
401	
402	\begin{proof}
403	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
404	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
405	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
406	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
407	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
408	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
409	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
410	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
411	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
412	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
413	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
414	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
415	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
416	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
417	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
418	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
419	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
420	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
421	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
422	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
423	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
424	
425	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
426	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
427	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
428	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
429	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
430	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
431	\end{proof}
432	
433	\begin{lemma}
434	
435	\leanok
436	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
437	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
438	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
439	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
440	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
441	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
442	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
443	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
444	  morphism
445	  \[
446	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
447	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
448	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
449	  \]
450	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
451	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
452	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
453	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
454	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
455	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
456	\end{lemma}
457	
458	\begin{proof}
459	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
460	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
461	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
462	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
463	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
464	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
465	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
466	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
467	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
468	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
469	  isomorphism}
470	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
471	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
472	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
473	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
474	  therefore extension of scalars along
475	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
476	
477	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
478	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
479	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
480	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the

554	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}

556	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
557	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
558	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
559	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
560	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
561	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
562	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
563	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
564	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
565	  left-whiskered morphism
566	  \[
567	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
568	  \]
569	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
570	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
571	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
572	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
573	\end{lemma}
574	
575	\begin{proof}
576	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
577	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
578	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
579	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
580	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
581	
582	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
583	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
584	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
585	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
586	  \[
587	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
588	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
589	  \]
590	
591	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
592	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
593	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
594	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
595	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
596	
597	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
598	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
599	  linear maps (Mathlib
600	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
601	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
602	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
603	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
604	
605	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
606	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
607	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
608	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
609	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
610	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
611	\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Sheafification inverts a localizer morphism of presheaves of modules]
  \label{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}
  \lean{PresheafOfModules.isIso_sheafification_map_of_W}
  Let \(R_0\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a
  Grothendieck topology \(J\), let \(R_{sh}\) be its sheafification, and let
  \(\alpha : R_0 \to R_{sh}.\mathrm{obj}\) be the (locally bijective) comparison
  map of presheaves of rings. Let \(f : A \to B\) be a morphism of presheaves of
  \(R_0\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0).\mathrm{map}\,f\) lies in the sheafification
  localizer class \(J.W\) --- equivalently, is locally bijective for \(J\). Then
  the presheaf-of-modules sheafification functor
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\,\alpha\) sends \(f\) to an
  isomorphism:
  \[
    (\mathtt{sheafification}\,\alpha).\mathrm{map}\,f
    \quad\text{is an isomorphism.}
  \]
  This is the go/no-go bridge consumed by the two absorption steps of the
  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: it converts ``\,whiskered unit lies in
  \(J.W\)\,'' into ``\,its sheafification is invertible\,''.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  This is a one-morphism reading of the structural identity that the
  sheafification of presheaves of \(R_0\)-modules \emph{is} the localization of
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R_0\) at the class
  \(J.W.\mathtt{inverseImage}\,(\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0)\) of morphisms whose
  underlying map of presheaves of abelian groups is locally bijective. Concretely,
  the underlying \(\mathtt{AddCommGrp}\)-valued sheafification inverts exactly the
  locally bijective morphisms, and Mathlib's
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.inverseImage\_W\_toPresheaf\_eq\_inverseImage\_%
  isomorphisms}\) identifies the preimage of \(J.W\) under
  \(\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0\) with the preimage of the class of isomorphisms under
  the module-level sheafification functor. Reading that equality of morphism
  classes at the single morphism \(f\): the hypothesis
  \(J.W\,\bigl((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R_0).\mathrm{map}\,f\bigr)\) places \(f\) in
  that preimage, hence \((\mathtt{sheafification}\,\alpha).\mathrm{map}\,f\) is an
  isomorphism. No new construction is required beyond instantiating the existing
  Mathlib identity at one morphism; this lemma is the thin wrapper used by the
  absorption steps of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}.
\end{proof}

  surjective (\(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)), with no hypothesis on

  trivialising cover.} Local injectivity asks that the equalizer sieve of the
  morphism be a covering sieve; this is a \emph{local-on-target} condition, so it

  (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}), there is an open cover \(\{V \hookrightarrow U\}\)

  \[
    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
      \;=\; a\Bigl(\, a\bigl(M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}\bigr)^{\flat}

          \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
        a\bigl(\, (M^{\flat} \otimes^{p} N^{\flat}) \otimes^{p} P^{\flat} \,\bigr).
      \]
